content.text: Distributed systems and external APIs fail transiently. A network packet is dropped, a server is briefly overloaded, a rate-limit quota resets every sixty seconds.
is the practice of automatically re-issuing a failed request after a short pause.
is the strategy that governs how long to wait between attempts: the delay doubles with each consecutive failure, giving the upstream service time to recover before the next attempt arrives.

For agentic AI systems, which routinely call LLM APIs, vector databases, tool endpoints, and orchestration services, transient failures are not edge cases — they are a steady-state reality at production scale. An agent that aborts on the first

response will fail far more often than necessary. A well-tuned retry layer silently absorbs the vast majority of these failures, delivering the reliability that users expect without surfacing errors that the code itself could resolve.
The canonical exponential backoff formula adds a random component called
to the delay: rather than every caller waiting exactly 2 seconds after the first failure, each waits a slightly different random duration drawn from
. This desynchronises retries from multiple concurrent callers that all failed at the same moment — a phenomenon called the
— and smooths the resulting traffic spike against the recovering service.

LLM APIs enforce per-minute and per-day token quotas that shift dynamically based on tier, model, and server load. An agentic pipeline that fans out ten parallel sub-tasks will almost certainly saturate a rate limit on busy requests. Without retries, the first
response propagates as an unhandled exception up the call stack, aborting a workflow that may have already spent several minutes and meaningful token budget on earlier steps.

Beyond rate limits, cloud APIs routinely return 5xx errors during rolling deploys, brief overload events, or infrastructure maintenance windows. The Anthropic API, AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, and every other major LLM provider document these as

errors — errors that a well-behaved client should retry. A production agent that does not retry these errors will fail in ways that look like product bugs to users but are actually infrastructure events that would have resolved within a few seconds.

Retries matter most in long-running agentic workflows. A research agent with a twenty-step pipeline or a multi-agent orchestrator spawning dozens of workers cannot tolerate even a 1% per-request failure rate without retry logic. At 1% failure probability per step, a 50-step pipeline fails entirely with probability roughly 40%. With one retry per step at the same underlying failure rate, that collapses to under 1%.
The caller receives a successful result regardless of whether the first, second, or third attempt succeeded. Upstream blips become invisible to application code.
In a multi-step agent workflow, a retry at step 15 avoids discarding the 14 steps already completed successfully.
Users see fewer errors. The system self-heals from intermittent network and rate-limit events that are invisible in aggregate metrics.
Retries are entirely client-side. No changes to the API, the server, or the infrastructure are required.
Retries compose cleanly with circuit breakers (which pause retries when a service is persistently down), timeouts (which bound single-attempt duration), and bulkheads (which isolate failure domains).
Retry counts, delay distributions, and eventual-success rates are straightforward to instrument and alert on, giving clear observability into transient error rates.
Retries are not free. Each retry consumes quota against a rate-limited API and delays the response seen by the caller. A retry policy configured with a high maximum-attempt count and short delays can

load on an already struggling service, turning a recoverable overload into a sustained DDoS from well-intentioned clients. This is why backoff (growing delays) and jitter (desynchronised delays) are essential companions to retries.

operations. A request that has a side effect — debiting a bank account, sending an email, creating a database record — must not be retried naively. If the original request succeeded but the response was lost in transit, a retry will trigger the effect a second time. For non-idempotent operations, the client must use idempotency keys or check for existing state before retrying.
A request that takes three attempts adds the wait times of the first two failures. In tail-latency-sensitive paths this can be significant.

During a prolonged API outage, a large fleet of agents all retrying simultaneously generates a traffic spike precisely when the service is trying to recover. Circuit breakers and adaptive concurrency limits mitigate this.
Does not help with non-retryable errors.
from a malformed prompt or a
from a missing API key are deterministic — retrying will never help and wastes time and quota.

A high retry rate is a symptom of a deeper issue: misconfigured rate limits, a slow LLM call taking too long, or an overloaded downstream. Retries that succeed are a signal to investigate, not a reason to stop caring.
State management in long agent loops.

If retrying an LLM call in the middle of a multi-turn conversation, the caller must be careful that partial results from the failed attempt are not appended to the conversation history before the retry is issued.

The core algorithm is simple: wrap the call in a loop, catch specific exception types or status codes, compute a delay, sleep, and try again. The subtlety is entirely in the delay schedule and the retry predicate.
for capped exponential backoff with full jitter is:
is the zero-indexed attempt number (0 for the first retry),
is the initial delay (commonly 0.5–1 second for LLM APIs),
is the maximum delay ceiling (commonly 30–60 seconds), and
is an additive random offset that desynchronises concurrent callers. A common choice is
: the actual delay is uniformly sampled from
once the exponential component saturates the cap.
The sequence of median delays (before jitter) for
is: n=0 → 1s, n=1 → 2s, n=2 → 4s, n=3 → 8s, n=4 → 16s, n=5 → 32s, n=6 → 60s (capped). With full jitter each actual sleep is a uniform draw in
, so the average delay is half the capped value.
determine which errors are worth retrying. The standard categories are:
The server explicitly says "slow down". Retry after the
header value if present, otherwise use the backoff formula.
: the request never reached the server. Retry is always safe.
400, 401, 403, 404, 422: these indicate a client-side problem that will not resolve on retry. Raise immediately.
Many APIs — including Anthropic's — return a

header on 429 responses specifying the number of seconds to wait. A well-behaved client reads this header and respects it, even if it is shorter or longer than the backoff formula would compute. Respecting
signals good citizenship to the provider and avoids consuming quota with retries that will fail again.

attrs.alt: Retry with exponential backoff flow: Agent sends request through Retry Logic (attempt counter, delay formula, retryable check, max retries) to the API. Attempt 1 returns 429, wait ~1s; Attempt 2 returns 503, wait ~2s; Attempt 3 returns 200 OK. Formula: delay(n) = min(max_wait, base x 2^n) + random(0, jitter).

content.text: The diagram shows the three layers in a retry-enabled call stack. The
is the application code that issues requests — it calls a function and awaits a result, unaware of whether the response came on the first attempt or the fourth. The
layer wraps that call, maintaining an attempt counter, computing delays using the exponential backoff formula, classifying errors as retryable or not, and enforcing the maximum-attempt limit. The

is the external service. On the first two attempts it returns transient error codes (429 and 503). On the third attempt it returns 200 OK, and the result propagates back to the agent as if the call had always succeeded.

The delay boxes (amber) represent the backoff sleep periods. Notice they grow: approximately 1 second after the first failure, 2 seconds after the second. In practice, with full jitter, these are random draws from
respectively, so concurrent agents that all hit the 429 at the same moment scatter their retries across the window rather than thundering back simultaneously.

Retry with exponential backoff is appropriate for any call to an external HTTP API that the application code cannot directly control. For agentic AI systems, this includes every call to an LLM inference API, a vector database, a web search tool, a code execution sandbox, or any micro-service that can transiently refuse requests.
the error is transient: 429, 5xx, network timeout. These errors resolve on their own without any change to the request.
Use a short base delay (0.5–1s) and tight cap (30s)
for interactive agent tasks where latency matters to the user.
Use a longer base (1–2s) and higher cap (120s)
for batch processing pipelines that run unattended and where throughput matters more than any single request's latency.

the error is deterministic: 400 Bad Request (fix the prompt), 401 Unauthorized (fix the key), 403 Forbidden (check permissions), 404 Not Found (check the endpoint), 422 Unprocessable Entity (fix the payload).
without an idempotency key. If the side effect already occurred, a naive retry duplicates it.
when the service is persistently down. Unbounded retries during a multi-hour outage hold resources and delay the agent's failure visibility.
List every outbound HTTP call in the agent. Classify each as: always idempotent (LLM reads, vector searches, tool reads) or potentially non-idempotent (writes, sends, mutations with side effects).

is the standard choice. It handles async/await, exception classification, delay strategies, and stop conditions out of the box. Alternatively, the Anthropic Python SDK retries 429 and 5xx automatically when
Explicitly list which status codes and exception types trigger a retry. Avoid catching
broadly — that would retry programming errors.
appropriate to the API's SLA and the pipeline's latency budget. The Anthropic API recommends a base of 0.5s and a cap of 60s.
header, wait at least that many seconds, even if the backoff formula would compute a shorter delay.
Emit a structured log entry on each retry attempt: attempt number, error code, delay chosen, and endpoint. This turns retry rate into an observable metric.
Retries should not run forever. Set a maximum elapsed time (e.g. 5 minutes for interactive tasks, 30 minutes for batch) beyond which the operation fails with a meaningful error to the caller.
Basic: Custom Retry with Exponential Backoff

import time
import random
import httpx

# Simple hand-rolled exponential backoff with full jitter
RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES = {429, 500, 502, 503, 504}

def retry_with_backoff(
    fn,
    *,
    max_retries: int = 5,
    base: float = 1.0,
    cap: float = 60.0,
):
    """
    Call fn(). On a retryable error, sleep for an exponentially growing
    random delay and try again, up to max_retries times.

    Returns the result of fn() on the first successful call.
    Raises the last exception if all retries are exhausted.
    """
    for attempt in range(max_retries + 1):
        try:
            return fn()
        except httpx.HTTPStatusError as exc:
            if attempt == max_retries or exc.response.status_code not in RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES:
                raise
            # Respect Retry-After header when present
            retry_after = exc.response.headers.get("Retry-After")
            if retry_after:
                delay = float(retry_after)
            else:
                # Full jitter: uniform sample in [0, min(cap, base * 2^attempt)]
                ceiling = min(cap, base * (2 ** attempt))
                delay = random.uniform(0, ceiling)
            print(f"Attempt {attempt + 1} failed ({exc.response.status_code}), retrying in {delay:.2f}s")
            time.sleep(delay)
        except (httpx.ConnectError, httpx.ReadTimeout) as exc:
            if attempt == max_retries:
                raise
            ceiling = min(cap, base * (2 ** attempt))
            delay = random.uniform(0, ceiling)
            print(f"Attempt {attempt + 1} network error ({type(exc).__name__}), retrying in {delay:.2f}s")
            time.sleep(delay)
    # unreachable, but satisfies type checkers
    raise RuntimeError("Exhausted retries")

# Usage
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key="sk-...")

def call_llm():
    return client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise the history of the internet in two sentences."}],
    )

response = retry_with_backoff(call_llm, max_retries=5, base=0.5, cap=60.0)
print(response.content[0].text)

import asyncio
import random
import anthropic
from tenacity import (
    retry,
    retry_if_exception,
    stop_after_attempt,
    wait_random_exponential,
    before_sleep_log,
)
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Retry predicate: only retry on transient API errors
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def is_transient(exc: BaseException) -> bool:
    if isinstance(exc, anthropic.RateLimitError):
        return True
    if isinstance(exc, anthropic.APIStatusError) and exc.status_code in (500, 502, 503, 504):
        return True
    if isinstance(exc, anthropic.APIConnectionError):
        return True
    return False


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Async LLM call with tenacity retry
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
@retry(
    retry=retry_if_exception(is_transient),
    wait=wait_random_exponential(multiplier=0.5, min=0.5, max=60),
    stop=stop_after_attempt(6),
    before_sleep=before_sleep_log(logger, logging.WARNING),
    reraise=True,  # re-raise the last exception on exhaustion
)
async def call_llm_with_retry(
    client: anthropic.AsyncAnthropic,
    messages: list[dict],
    model: str = "claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens: int = 1024,
) -> str:
    """
    Call the Anthropic messages API with automatic exponential backoff.

    Retries on RateLimitError, 5xx errors, and connection errors.
    Raises immediately on 4xx client errors (bad request, auth failure).
    Returns the first text block from the response.
    """
    response = await client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        messages=messages,
    )
    return response.content[0].text


async def main():
    async_client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic(api_key="sk-...")

    text = await call_llm_with_retry(
        async_client,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is exponential backoff?"}],
    )
    print(text)

asyncio.run(main())

Consider a production research agent that takes a user question, issues ten concurrent web-search and LLM sub-tasks in parallel, and synthesises a final report. At moderate concurrency, some of the sub-tasks will encounter 429 errors from the LLM API. Without retries, those sub-tasks fail, the orchestrator receives partial results, and the report is incomplete or the whole pipeline raises an exception. With retry logic wrapping each sub-task, the 429s are absorbed transparently.

import asyncio
import anthropic
from tenacity import (
    retry,
    retry_if_exception,
    stop_after_attempt,
    wait_random_exponential,
)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Retry decorator (reused across all agent tool calls)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def _is_transient(exc: BaseException) -> bool:
    if isinstance(exc, anthropic.RateLimitError):
        return True
    if isinstance(exc, anthropic.APIStatusError) and exc.status_code >= 500:
        return True
    if isinstance(exc, anthropic.APIConnectionError):
        return True
    return False

llm_retry = retry(
    retry=retry_if_exception(_is_transient),
    wait=wait_random_exponential(multiplier=1.0, min=1.0, max=60.0),
    stop=stop_after_attempt(5),
    reraise=True,
)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Single sub-task: generate a section of the report
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
@llm_retry
async def generate_section(client: anthropic.AsyncAnthropic, topic: str, section: str) -> str:
    """
    Generate one section of a research report for `topic`.
    Decorated with llm_retry so transient API failures are silently retried.
    """
    response = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=800,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"You are a research assistant writing a report on: {topic}\n"
                f"Write the '{section}' section in 3-4 concise paragraphs. "
                "Cite only publicly known facts. Do not invent citations."
            ),
        }],
    )
    return response.content[0].text


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Orchestrator: fan out ten sections in parallel
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
async def research(topic: str, api_key: str) -> dict[str, str]:
    client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic(api_key=api_key)

    sections = [
        "Introduction",
        "Historical background",
        "Core concepts",
        "Current state of the art",
        "Key challenges",
        "Recent research directions",
        "Industry applications",
        "Ethical considerations",
        "Future outlook",
        "Summary",
    ]

    # All ten sections run concurrently. Individually, each retries on transient errors.
    tasks = [generate_section(client, topic, s) for s in sections]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=False)
    return dict(zip(sections, results))


async def main():
    report = await research("Large language model alignment", api_key="sk-...")
    for section, content in report.items():
        print(f"=== {section} ===")
        print(content[:200], "...")
        print()

asyncio.run(main())

In a deployment running this pipeline at production load, typical metrics look like: 5–15% of individual sub-task calls hit a 429 on the first attempt; approximately 1% require a second retry; fewer than 0.05% reach a third attempt. Without retries, 5–15% of pipeline runs would fail. With retries configured as above, the success rate exceeds 99.9% — and the added latency is confined to the sub-tasks that actually encounter errors, which run in the background while unaffected sub-tasks proceed normally.
Retrying Non-Idempotent Operations Without Idempotency Keys

The most dangerous mistake is retrying a request that already executed its side effect. If an agent calls a payment API and the response is lost due to a network timeout, retrying the original request triggers a second charge. Always use idempotency keys for writes: send a UUID in the
header, and check whether the original request succeeded before retrying.
Not Capping the Delay or Adding Jitter

Without a cap, exponential backoff produces delays of 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, 32s, 64s, 128s… A sixth retry would wait over two minutes. For interactive tasks, a cap of 30–60 seconds is appropriate. Without a cap, a brief transient error leads to an unexpectedly long wait.
Without jitter, agents that all encounter the same 429 at the same moment will all retry after exactly the same delay, creating a synchronized traffic spike on the recovering service. Full jitter —
Retrying Inside an Agentic Loop Without Idempotent State

In a multi-turn agent loop, if an LLM call succeeds but the code crashes before appending the assistant turn to the conversation history, a naive retry re-sends the same messages list. This is safe — the LLM call is idempotent. However, if the assistant turn was partially appended before the crash, retrying sends a malformed history. Always append assistant turns atomically after a confirmed success, not speculatively before checking the response.
Define one retry decorator or utility function per project. All LLM calls and external API calls use the same decorator. Avoid duplicating retry parameters across files — a change to
must not require editing twenty call sites.
Use the SDK's built-in retry for the happy path.

parameter handles the common case with no extra code. Add a custom wrapper only when the SDK's built-in behaviour is insufficient (e.g. when the pipeline needs to react to retry events or needs longer caps).
header, sleep for at least that duration. Using a shorter backoff wastes quota on requests that will fail again.
Retries should not run indefinitely. Use
(tenacity) or an equivalent to cap the total elapsed time. Fail explicitly rather than silently blocking a pipeline thread forever.
Log at WARNING level on each retry.
Log the attempt number, delay, error code, and endpoint. Aggregate this as a metric (e.g. a counter
). Alert when the retry rate exceeds a threshold — it signals a misconfigured rate limit or unexpected traffic spike.
in unit tests to exercise the retry logic. A retry decorator that is never exercised in tests is untested code.

A per-attempt timeout (e.g. 30s per LLM call) and a retry budget (e.g. 5 attempts) are independent knobs. Set both. An LLM call that hangs for 90 seconds is not a retryable transient error — it is a timeout that should fail fast.

In a fan-out orchestrator, if 20 sub-tasks each retry up to 5 times, the orchestrator might issue 100 requests while waiting for results. Ensure the total request budget is compatible with the API tier's per-minute limit.

Retry logic has negligible CPU and memory cost — the dominant cost is the sleep time during backoff periods. The performance impact is felt through increased latency on affected requests and increased total API request volume.
, the expected additional latency introduced by one retry is approximately
. For two retries: 0.5s + 1s = 1.5s in expectation. For LLM APIs where inference itself takes 2–10 seconds, the retry overhead is typically a minority of total latency when failures are rare.

A system with 10,000 requests per hour, a 5% transient failure rate, and a maximum of 5 retries per failure will issue at most 10,000 × 1.05 = 10,500 requests, since most transient failures resolve on the first retry. However, during a sustained rate-limit event where 30% of requests fail and each requires 3 retries, volume amplifies to 10,000 × 1.9 ≈ 19,000. This must fit within the API tier limit — if it does not, the retry storm makes the rate-limit problem worse.
Token bucket and rate limit awareness.
When an agent is consistently hitting rate limits, the right fix is to reduce concurrency or upgrade the API tier — not to increase
. Retries only help with sporadic transient errors, not with sustained overload.
In async agents, retries sleep without blocking the event loop (when using
). Other sub-tasks continue making progress during a backoff wait, amortising the delay cost across the fan-out.
For batch pipelines, implement a semaphore to cap concurrency. Retrying coroutines hold concurrency slots during backoff; a semaphore ensures a burst of failures does not block new work indefinitely.
Decorator API, async support, configurable predicates, jitter, logging hooks
Custom retry logic in async agents
Built-in, zero config, handles 429 and 5xx automatically
Quick setup with no custom requirements
Decorator-based, exponential and Fibonacci strategies, on_backoff callback
Typed, async-first, built on tenacity, adds instrumentation hooks
Production async services needing metrics integration
HTTP-level retry with configurable status codes and backoff
Low-level HTTP clients that bypass the SDK
For most Python agentic AI projects, the recommended approach is to use the
on the client) for the default case, and add

for custom retry logic where the built-in behaviour is insufficient — for example, when the pipeline needs to log retry events, use a different backoff curve, or coordinate retries across a group of parallel sub-tasks.
Re-issues failed requests with growing delays
Transient API errors, rate limits, network blips
Opens after N failures; stops all requests temporarily
Persistent service failures; protecting a struggling downstream
Adds latency when the breaker trips spuriously
Aborts slow requests immediately; no retry
Real-time paths where stale results are worse than no result
Failed tasks are stored for async replay
Non-real-time batch jobs that can tolerate minutes-hour delays
Not suitable for synchronous agent queries
Switches to a cheaper or cached response on failure
When approximate results are better than waiting
Answer quality may degrade; fallback must be maintained
Persists workflow state; auto-resumes after crash
Long-running workflows spanning hours or days
Heavy infrastructure; overkill for simple API calls
Retries and circuit breakers are complementary, not alternatives. A robust production agent uses
for transient failures (the request will succeed on the next attempt) and a

for persistent failures (the service is down and retrying consumes resources without benefit). The circuit breaker opens after N consecutive failures, waits a probe interval, and then allows one request through to test recovery.
Retry logic, if misconfigured, can become an attack surface or a reliability hazard. Several patterns deserve careful attention in production.
Do not log sensitive data in retry events.

callback in tenacity receives the exception object. If the exception message contains API keys, user data, or prompt text, logging it verbatim exposes sensitive information in log aggregators. Log only the status code, attempt number, and endpoint, not the full exception body.
Validate API keys before entering the retry loop.
A missing or malformed API key returns 401 on every attempt — retrying just wastes quota and delays the error. Check for the presence of the key at startup, not at call time.
. Do not derive them from user-supplied input, which could allow a caller to collide keys intentionally.
Set a hard limit on total retries to avoid runaway agents.
An agent in an unmonitored background task that enters a retry loop during a prolonged outage will accumulate costs and consume concurrency slots. Use
to bound both attempt count and total elapsed time.
Monitor retry rate as a reliability signal.

A sudden increase in retry rate — even if retries eventually succeed — signals that the upstream service is degrading. Alert on retry rate exceeding 5% of total calls for more than five minutes. This gives an early warning before the retry layer is overwhelmed.
Use separate retry budgets for critical and non-critical paths.

A real-time user-facing query should retry aggressively (short delays, up to 5 attempts). A background batch job can retry less aggressively (longer delays, higher cap) since latency tolerance is higher. Sharing one retry configuration across both can cause the batch job to starve real-time paths of concurrency during high-load periods.
Transient failures — 429, 5xx, network timeouts — are routine at LLM API scale. Retry logic is not optional defensive coding; it is a prerequisite for production reliability in agentic systems.
. Capping bounds worst-case latency; full jitter desynchronises concurrent callers and prevents thundering-herd load spikes.
errors: 429, 5xx, connection errors. Never retry 4xx client errors (400, 401, 403, 404, 422) — they will fail identically on every attempt.
Retrying non-idempotent operations without idempotency keys duplicates side effects. Use
headers for writes, or verify whether the original request succeeded before issuing a retry.
parameter enables built-in exponential backoff with no additional code. Use it as the default; add tenacity only when custom behaviour is needed.

Always log retry events as structured metrics and alert when retry rate exceeds a threshold. High retry rate signals an underlying rate-limit or capacity problem that requires action beyond retry configuration.
to avoid blocking on hung connections, and a
to prevent runaway retry loops in unmonitored background agents.
